title: Episode 3

jokes.text: Fleabag looks at a jogger, says 'I should get on it.' Cut to [00:23]: 'Jogging.' — the punchline is delayed by 22 seconds of silence/walking
jokes.setup: Fleabag makes a vague statement of self-improvement that could mean anything
jokes.punchline: After a long beat, she clarifies: 'Jogging.' — the delay and mundanity deflate any gravitas
jokes.explanation: The comic timing IS the joke — the pause makes the anticlimactic reveal funnier. Character-consistent: Fleabag's self-deprecating relationship with self-improvement. Slight but effective opener.

jokes.text: Fleabag tells Claire she did a fart that 'was exactly like Mum's' — 'A door opening, or suspicious dark?' 'Door opening.'
jokes.setup: Two women are jogging together — a potentially ordinary sisterly chat
jokes.punchline: The conversation immediately turns to categorizing farts by similarity to their dead mother's, with Claire deploying a highly specific taxonomy ('door opening' vs 'suspicious dark')
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. The taxonomy ('door opening' vs 'suspicious dark') is a specific, original comic construction that only two sisters this close and this strange could use. It smuggles grief — these women have inherited their dead mother's fart signature — into absurdist bodily humor with complete naturalness. Claire's instant, authoritative fluency with the categories reveals a whole lifetime of intimate, weird sisterly history. Justifies 8+ because the specificity is genuinely inventive and the tonal collision (grief + bodily comedy + warmth) is masterfully calibrated.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'My farts used to be like, "Pah!" Now they're just sort of fighting their way out.' Claire: 'I haven't farted in about three years.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag describes her farts with vivid physical specificity and a degree of regret
jokes.punchline: Claire's deadpan, clinically delivered claim that she hasn't farted in three years — delivered as a simple statement of fact, implying extraordinary sphincter control or denial
jokes.explanation: Claire's line is a perfect character joke — her emotional and physical repression externalized as an impossible bodily claim delivered with zero self-awareness or irony. The contrast with Fleabag's confessional openness crystallizes both characters in one exchange. Highly quotable and deeply character-specific. Earns 8+ for character integration and economy — four words ('three years', delivered as bald fact) do enormous character work.

jokes.text: Birthday cake presented; Claire says 'She won't eat it.' Fleabag says 'Thanks.'
jokes.setup: Someone presents a birthday cake as a kind gesture
jokes.punchline: Claire immediately predicts the recipient won't eat it — simultaneously dismissive and probably accurate — while Fleabag's 'Thanks' is ambiguously directed and deflective
jokes.explanation: A quick, functional joke that establishes Claire's controlling nature and hints at a difficult relationship with the birthday person. Works but not distinguished.

jokes.text: Claire's elaborate instructions for the party: '7pm arrival for a 7.30 surprise,' 'just wear trousers,' 'don't drink too much,' 'it's basically a business meeting.' Fleabag: 'Sounds like a blast.'
jokes.setup: Claire gives instructions for what is ostensibly a birthday party
jokes.punchline: The instructions escalate in joylessness — wardrobe restrictions, alcohol restrictions, rebranding as a 'business meeting' — until Fleabag's dry 'Sounds like a blast' lands as understatement
jokes.explanation: Claire's escalating party-killing instructions are perfectly in character and the accumulation works. 'Just wear trousers' is a funny specific detail. Fleabag's comeback is economical. Strong character-comedy beat.

jokes.text: Fleabag asks 'Can I bring a date?' Claire asks 'Harry?' Fleabag says 'No.' Claire: 'Who?' Fleabag: 'Oh, I don't know yet.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks to bring a date to the party, implying she has someone in mind
jokes.punchline: She doesn't know who yet — the confidence with which she announced a date collapses into complete openness, revealing her chaotic romantic life
jokes.explanation: The beat captures Fleabag's bravado covering her loneliness/chaos perfectly. The rhythm — Claire's expectant 'Who?' then the beat before 'Oh, I don't know yet' — is well-crafted.

jokes.text: Fleabag says 'It's really inappropriate to jog around a graveyard.' Claire: 'Why?' Fleabag: 'Flaunting your life.'
jokes.setup: They are jogging through a graveyard; Fleabag comments on the impropriety
jokes.punchline: 'Flaunting your life' — reframes the jogging as an act of insensitivity toward the dead, a genuinely novel and dark comic logic
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's standout line. Three words — 'Flaunting your life' — construct an entirely original comic perspective: that physical vitality is an act of social rudeness in the presence of the dead. It is dark, perfectly economical, and the comedic logic is airtight. The word 'flaunting' does all the work, turning a neutral activity into a moral transgression. This is comparable to the 'Never go with a hippie' anchor in economy and unexpected specificity, and surpasses it in tonal complexity given the show's grief themes.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'God, I can't wait to be old.' Claire: 'If it's any consolation, you look older than you are.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag expresses longing for old age (presumably for its freedoms or permissions)
jokes.punchline: Claire offers this as 'consolation' — framing a mild insult as a gift, delivered with complete sincerity or deadpan cruelty, character-ambiguously
jokes.explanation: The joke works because 'if it's any consolation' sets up a comfort, then delivers an insult. The cruelty is very Claire — the ambiguity of whether it's malicious or oblivious makes it richer. Strong character-comedy.

jokes.text: Claire takes a phone call mid-conversation; Fleabag turns to camera to give the exposition about their dead mother — but the information 'it was particularly hard, cos she had amazing boobs' undercuts the grief with comedy
jokes.setup: Fleabag begins a direct-address monologue about her mother's death, establishing the emotional stakes
jokes.punchline: The grief pivot: '...it was particularly hard, cos she had amazing boobs' — the implied tragedy is the loss of aesthetic excellence rather than the person
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash between 'double mastectomy, never really recovered' and 'cos she had amazing boobs' is a signature Fleabag move — grief and vanity colliding in a way that feels true rather than tasteless. Character-specific because only Fleabag would deliver this so matter-of-factly.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'She used to tell me I was lucky cos mine would never get in the way. My sister's got whoppers, but she got all of Mum's good bits.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is describing their mother's body and legacy
jokes.punchline: The sisterly comparison positions Claire as having received the genetic inheritance while Fleabag got the consolation prize — delivered as an almost-casual grievance
jokes.explanation: Competent character comedy that extends the mother's-body thread while establishing sibling rivalry. 'She got all of Mum's good bits' is a solid, slightly rueful punchline.

jokes.text: Fleabag asks what Martin has 'given' Claire; Claire says 'A cursory stroke would be nice.' Fleabag: 'What? No bang-bang?'
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks about Claire's sex life with Martin, framed sympathetically
jokes.punchline: 'Bang-bang' — Fleabag's juvenile euphemism for sex is funny in its infantilism, and 'cursory stroke' is a perfect understatement for Claire's resigned sexual expectation
jokes.explanation: 'Cursory stroke' is genuinely funny in its Britishness and specificity — it's both resigned and precise. 'Bang-bang' is an effective contrast in register. The exchange builds the Martin-is-terrible thread.

jokes.text: Claire struggles to say 'penis' in the graveyard while Fleabag coaches her like a child: 'Come on, little one. Come on, please.' — Claire finally says it and Fleabag says 'Thank you.'
jokes.setup: Claire is trying to describe a problem with Martin involving his penis
jokes.punchline: Claire, an adult professional woman, cannot say the word 'penis' in a graveyard to her own sister, requiring coaxing — the reversal of the expected adult conversation
jokes.explanation: The comedy comes entirely from character: Claire's repression is so total she can't say 'penis' to her own sister while discussing her marriage. Fleabag's 'Come on, little one' inverts the age dynamic beautifully. The 'Thank you' is a perfect, exhausted Fleabag cap. Strong character-integration score justified.

jokes.text: Fleabag spots a sobbing man: 'Christ, look at that man. Tragic.' Claire: 'Nah, he's a con.' — 'You can't call someone who is grieving a con.'
jokes.setup: A man is openly, dramatically sobbing at a grave
jokes.punchline: Fleabag, who the show is built around grief, immediately calls him a faker — which is both hypocritical and sharp
jokes.explanation: The joke works on a character level — Fleabag's cynicism about public grief is darkly ironic given her own buried grief. Claire's moral objection is the straight-man foil. Good tonal layering.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'No-one grieves like that unless they are in a film or from Italy.'
jokes.setup: The debate about whether the sobbing man is authentic
jokes.punchline: The Italy specificity — genuine grief is national, not universal — is an absurdly confident cultural generalization delivered as fact
jokes.explanation: 'Or from Italy' is the joke — the specificity elevates what could be a throwaway dismissal into something wry and quotable. The confident absurdity of the cultural claim is very Fleabag.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'Trust me, he's at a different grave every day, he can't get enough of it.' — immediately followed by the man sobbing audibly
jokes.setup: Fleabag doubles down that he's a grief tourist who visits multiple graves
jokes.punchline: The man's genuine-sounding sobs immediately follow, providing auditory contradiction — reality punishes her cynicism in real time
jokes.explanation: The immediate auditory contradiction of Fleabag's cynicism is a clean comedic structure. The sound-design timing is doing a lot of work here.

jokes.text: The grieving man turns out to actually visit different graves — 'You come here every day?' — confirming Fleabag was right
jokes.setup: Fleabag's theory seemed undermined by his genuine sobbing
jokes.punchline: She was completely right — he does come every day, he is a grief aficionado — turning Fleabag into a reliable narrator about grief-fraud, darkly
jokes.explanation: The double-reversal is effective: the sob seemed to prove Fleabag wrong, then the confirmation proves her right. It rewards viewers who tracked the beat. The rewatch bonus is for the layered grief-recognition irony — she can spot fake grief because she knows how real grief works.

jokes.text: Fleabag instructing on party behavior: 'Don't do a jumpy-outy surprise thing, and don't sing Happy Birthday, I couldn't bear it.'
jokes.setup: Party instructions being given
jokes.punchline: 'I couldn't bear it' — the specific horror of the 'jumpy-outy surprise thing' and Happy Birthday is disproportionate, implying profound social sensitivity
jokes.explanation: 'Jumpy-outy surprise thing' is a pleasingly childish coinage. 'I couldn't bear it' as applied to Happy Birthday is gently funny. Competent scene-setting humor.

jokes.text: Fleabag says to camera: 'I'm, erm... I'm actually looking forward to it.' — beat of surprise at her own admission
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been complaining about everything; now she admits she's looking forward to the party
jokes.punchline: The hesitation and surprise in her own voice — she discovers the feeling as she says it, which is both funny and emotionally loaded
jokes.explanation: A quiet but effective fourth-wall moment where Fleabag's self-awareness catches her off-guard. The comedy is in the vulnerability, not a punchline. Rewatch bonus because it gains meaning knowing how the episode ends.

jokes.text: Fleabag calls Harry (her ex) to invite him to Claire's party, fumbling the message across multiple beats, ending with a pathetic '...birthday party. Anyway, give me a call, and I hope you're OK. Bye.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag said she'd find a date who wasn't Harry; she immediately calls Harry
jokes.punchline: The voicemail dissolves into inarticulate hesitation and ends in affectionate concern for him — showing she still cares deeply, undercutting her bravado
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'I don't know yet' is paid off immediately with Harry's voicemail — she knew exactly who. The vulnerability of the broken-down voicemail is where the comedy lives: the pauses, the deflation, the 'I hope you're OK.' Character-specific and honest.

jokes.text: 'I can't go out with a dog.' — Fleabag apparently photographing herself with or walking a dog, seemingly as a dating profile ploy
jokes.setup: Context unclear from transcript alone but implies Fleabag is trying to use a dog as a social/dating prop
jokes.punchline: The matter-of-fact self-awareness that using a dog as an accessory won't work for her specific situation
jokes.explanation: Without visual context this reads weakly. Likely funnier on screen depending on what 'a dog' refers to. Transcript-only score must be lower.

jokes.text: CAMERA CLICKS — Fleabag is apparently taking photos for an ex who demanded vagina pictures from wherever she was, 10-11 times a day, including while temping
jokes.setup: Fleabag begins describing an ex-boyfriend's request for daily vagina pictures
jokes.punchline: The sheer volume ('ten or 11 times a day'), the specificity of location ('one day when I was temping'), and the trail-off implying she did it — the mundane workplace setting colliding with the absurd demand
jokes.explanation: The comedy escalates through specificity — '10 or 11 times a day' is funnier than 'constantly' because the precision implies he had a schedule. The office setting is an inspired context for the absurd demand. The trail-off to [05:06] without conclusion is itself a comedic structure — we never find out what happened when she was temping.

jokes.text: 'Time to throw the net out.' — Fleabag addresses the camera, apparently at a bar or social venue, announcing she's going to find a date
jokes.setup: Fleabag needs a date for tonight
jokes.punchline: The fishing metaphor delivered with weary pragmatism — not romantic excitement but strategic deployment
jokes.explanation: Functional scene-transition joke. The net metaphor applied to dating is tired but delivered with the right weary energy.

jokes.text: Martin is drunk at a shop, demands Fleabag 'Smack me in the face. Really hard.' Fleabag obliges. Martin: 'Fuck. I think you gave me a semi.'
jokes.setup: Martin is behaving erratically; Fleabag is attempting to manage him
jokes.punchline: The face-slap producing sexual arousal — revealing Martin's masochism while making Fleabag an unwitting sexual participant
jokes.explanation: Martin's sexual inappropriateness is well-established and this beat escalates it into physical territory. 'I think you gave me a semi' is a horrible and perfect Martinism — simultaneously oversharing, boundary-violating, and delivered as a compliment.

jokes.text: Fleabag's monologue about Martin being 'Fun Drunk Martin' — 'no-one wants to admit there's a problem because then they don't get to have crazy nights out with Fun Drunk Martin'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is trying to explain why no one confronts Martin about his drinking
jokes.punchline: 'Fun Drunk Martin' as a brand — the social enabling of problem drinking through the myth of the charismatic drunk
jokes.explanation: 'Fun Drunk Martin' as a proper noun is the joke — transforming an individual's dysfunction into a social institution. Sharp, recognizable observation with genuine comic clarity.

jokes.text: Fleabag's monologue: Martin is 'one of those men who is explosively sexually inappropriate with everyone, but makes you feel bad if you take offence because he was "just being fun"'
jokes.setup: Description of Martin's social behavior
jokes.punchline: The 'just being fun' defense — naming and skewering a specific, recognizable type of male social predation with precision
jokes.explanation: Sharp cultural observation that lands as comedy and social commentary simultaneously. The 'just being fun' phrase is doing precise work — it names the exact rhetorical shield such men deploy. This is observational comedy at its best: specific, recognizable, and slightly uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'Honestly, you could tell him you're going to pop to the loo and he'd say...' Martin: 'Yes, you pop to the loo, pull down your knickers, and then I will come in and fuck you.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is illustrating Martin's pervasive sexual inappropriateness with a hypothetical
jokes.punchline: Martin immediately demonstrates by saying exactly that — in excessive detail, unprompted, while in a shop — the hypothetical becomes real in real time
jokes.explanation: The structure is excellent: Fleabag sets up a hypothetical illustration, then Martin makes it literal. The joke is that the hypothetical required no exaggeration — it's just Tuesday for Martin. The directness of his statement, its clinical detail, and the public setting make it cringe-comedy gold. The structure justifies a higher craft score.

jokes.text: In a pet shop: 'Does anyone ever come here? I mean, it is creepy as fuck.'
jokes.setup: They've entered a pet shop, apparently unusual or run-down
jokes.punchline: Martin's direct, slightly aggressive assessment of the shop — his bluntness is applied to an innocent business
jokes.explanation: Functional texture joke establishing the pet shop as creepy. Not distinguished but earns a mild laugh.

jokes.text: Pet shop owner: 'Why don't you get her a guinea pig? It was a surprise hit here.' Fleabag: 'What, you think she'd like a pig? Can I take this one?' Owner: 'No, not that one. Christ, woman, there's something wrong with that one, it's got death in its eyes.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is choosing a gift guinea pig
jokes.punchline: 'It's got death in its eyes' — the specific guinea pig is pre-disqualified on existential grounds
jokes.explanation: Rewatch bonus because the guinea pig with 'death in its eyes' ends up being exactly the one acquired — adding retrospective dark comedy. 'Death in its eyes' is a vivid, memorable phrase applied to a guinea pig.

jokes.text: Pet shop owner: 'Vet says she's depressed.' Fleabag: 'Oh.' Beat. 'Aren't we all, girl?'
jokes.setup: The condemned guinea pig has a clinical diagnosis
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's 'Aren't we all, girl?' — solidarity with a depressed guinea pig, delivered as a genuine moment of identification
jokes.explanation: 'Aren't we all, girl?' is four words that do extraordinary work — comedy of recognition (who can't relate?), character revelation (Fleabag's own depression beneath the bravado), and cross-species empathy. The economy is exceptional. This is Fleabag's brand of comedy at its most efficient: funny and genuinely touching simultaneously.

jokes.text: Pet shop owner: 'You know, guinea pigs can die of loneliness.' Fleabag: 'Can they?' — beat of resonance
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just identified with the depressed guinea pig
jokes.punchline: The information that guinea pigs die of loneliness lands on Fleabag who is herself lonely — the joke is the weight of the fact rather than its content
jokes.explanation: The callback builds the guinea pig sequence into something genuinely moving. The comedy is in Fleabag's 'Can they?' — a simple two-word question that carries enormous subtext. The show's signature move: comedy that is also grief.

jokes.text: Martin trying to buy Claire a present: 'I bought her a necklace with her name on it, that she found and told me not to buy.' ... 'I bought her a book that she already has.' ... 'She says not to buy any clothes because she probably won't wear them.' ... 'She scares me!'
jokes.setup: Martin is explaining the gift-buying nightmare
jokes.punchline: Each gift attempt ends in specific failure; the escalation ends in Martin's honest admission: 'She scares me!' — the most relatable and vulnerable thing he's said
jokes.explanation: The escalating gift failures are funny by accumulation, and 'She scares me!' is a perfect cap — Martin's bravado collapses into honest fear of Claire. The specificity of each failed gift (the preemptive necklace veto) makes it land.

jokes.text: Fleabag and Martin negotiating a consulting fee: 'Pay me and I'll help you.' 'Fuck off.' 'How much?' '£60.' '70.' 'Done.' 'Yeah!'
jokes.setup: Fleabag offers to help Martin buy Claire a gift in exchange for payment
jokes.punchline: Martin counterbids up his own price — he negotiates against himself — and Fleabag's delighted 'Yeah!' suggests she was bluffing with the lower number
jokes.explanation: The rapid-fire rhythm of the negotiation is well-executed. Martin overbidding himself is the main joke — revealing he's either drunk or just fundamentally incapable of strategy. Fleabag's 'Yeah!' is a perfect beat of triumph.

jokes.text: In what appears to be a boutique: Fleabag points at people as possible gift archetypes — 'I don't know who she is... No... What about...? No!'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is trying to help Martin identify what kind of woman Claire is
jokes.punchline: The rapid rejection of every option — implying Claire is genuinely unknowable or unsatisfiable
jokes.explanation: Likely funnier visually with the actual people being pointed at. Transcript-only this reads as texture rather than a complete joke.

jokes.text: 'Chic means boring - don't tell the French.'
jokes.setup: Martin suggests something 'chic' as a gift option
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's quick reframe that 'chic' is code for 'boring', with the conspiratorial aside to keep it from the French
jokes.explanation: Sharp and economical. Eight words that work as both a joke about the fashion word and a quick anti-French quip. 'Don't tell the French' is perfectly calibrated — enough edge to be funny, light enough to not be mean. Quotable.

jokes.text: 'Chic means boring — don't tell the French.'
jokes.setup: Martin or Fleabag describes something as 'chic'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's aside reframes 'chic' as a euphemism for boring, then diplomatically warns against spreading this to the French
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable, excellent economy, and the diplomatic 'don't tell the French' is charming. The logic is airtight: chic IS boring elevated to aspiration. Similar in spirit to the Italy joke (id 14) in its confident cultural generalization.

jokes.text: 'Stop checking, all right, nobody loves you. Help me here.'
jokes.setup: Martin (presumably) is checking his phone or his appearance
jokes.punchline: 'Nobody loves you' — a darkly funny, disproportionately brutal response to whatever Martin is doing
jokes.explanation: 'Nobody loves you' is a sharply delivered existential blow used to redirect attention — the comedy is in the casual cruelty and the 'Help me here' pivot that treats it as entirely unremarkable.

jokes.text: 'Get her something she'd never get herself. Surprise her.' / 'She'll think I've gone nuts.' / 'No, she'll think you see her as this person, and everyone wants to be this person.'
jokes.setup: Gift-buying philosophy discussion
jokes.punchline: 'Aren't these for children?' — Martin's deflating response to Fleabag's aspirational gift idea
jokes.explanation: Functional exchange. The 'aren't these for children?' puncturing of Fleabag's sentiment is a decent beat, though the surrounding dialogue is a bit meandering compared to the tighter jokes elsewhere.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'Get her something she'd never get herself. Surprise her.' Martin: 'She'll think I've gone nuts.' Fleabag: 'No, she'll think you see her as this person, and everyone wants to be this person.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is coaching Martin on how to buy a gift
jokes.punchline: Martin's immediate fear of being seen as 'nuts' for a thoughtful gesture — suggesting his baseline is so low that creativity seems pathological
jokes.explanation: The exchange works as character comedy — Fleabag has insight Martin can't access — and 'she'll think I've gone nuts' is a revealing fear. Functional but not outstanding.

jokes.text: LAUGHTER — apparently they've found something funny in the shop (likely drug-related or just giddy) — the scene break implies they've become intoxicated and giggly
jokes.setup: Fleabag and Martin shopping, earlier establishing they are already somewhat drunk/high
jokes.punchline: Pure laughter as a scene beat — the comedy is that we don't know what set them off, implying they've gone further down a rabbit hole
jokes.explanation: Texture moment — the laughter itself communicates their state without exposition. Functional bridging moment.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'I'm going to hold his face in my head so you can see him.' — then a beat — 'I'm not getting anything.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag claims to be able to telepathically transmit a face to her sister
jokes.punchline: Claire's 'I'm not getting anything' — she plays along completely, treating the psychic face-transmission as a real system that has simply failed to work
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional. The joke works on multiple levels: Fleabag's casual magical thinking, Claire's deadpan acceptance of the premise (she doesn't question the system, only its failure), and the follow-up 'Make your face his face' as a debugging attempt. It reveals a unique sisterly language of shared fantasy — they've invented and apparently used this system before. The comedy is in the utter seriousness with which both engage the absurd premise. Earns 8+ for originality and character integration: no other show and no other pair of sisters would play this exchange this way.

jokes.text: 'I'm not getting anything.' 'Make your face his face.' 'Yeah, OK.' — and then the face actually works
jokes.setup: The face transmission has failed
jokes.punchline: Fleabag instructs Claire to actively make her face his face — this apparently works, as confirmed by the next beat
jokes.explanation: 'Make your face his face' is a funny instruction — Claire presumably pulls a face meant to represent the neighbour. This is visual comedy relying on the transcript's suggestion of physical performance.

jokes.text: 'Is he mixed race? I'm getting mixed race.' Fleabag: 'Yes!'
jokes.setup: Claire is apparently receiving the psychic face transmission
jokes.punchline: The system appears to actually work — Claire gets something right — played completely straight
jokes.explanation: The callback elevates the absurdist premise by having it 'work' — committing fully to the bit. The comedy of the payoff is in the mutual delight: 'Yes!' Fleabag's genuine excitement that the face was received makes it both funnier and oddly sweet. The continuation into the man actually appearing at the door amplifies further.

jokes.text: DOOR CLOSES. The actual neighbour appears — Fleabag and Claire have apparently been staring at him telepathically, and immediately have to deal with him in person.
jokes.setup: Claire has just psychically identified the neighbour correctly
jokes.punchline: He immediately appears — the joke is the timing, the embarrassment of having been psychically studying a man who then walks in
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy of the timing — he appears the moment they've identified him — is perfectly executed. The embarrassment is compounded by the subsequent 'Go on. No. OK. OK!' implying mutual encouragement to speak to him.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'Jesus. I'm really sorry, we're really high. We just really wanted to know what you look like.'
jokes.setup: The neighbour appears and Fleabag has to explain their behavior
jokes.punchline: The honest, direct explanation — 'we just really wanted to know what you look like' — strips away all social pretense. Being high is offered as adequate explanation for the psychic-face-transmission project.
jokes.explanation: Fleabag's honesty in mortifying situations is her defining character trait — she tells the truth when lying would be easier. This is a perfect example: 'we just really wanted to know what you look like' is disarming, funny, and very Fleabag.

jokes.text: Claire (offscreen or deadpan): 'She thinks you're lovely.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has already embarrassed them both; Claire intervenes
jokes.punchline: Claire's non-apology — she doesn't apologize for the psychic stalking, she just appends a compliment as if that handles it — delivered in perfect Claire fashion
jokes.explanation: Five words that are completely, unmistakably Claire. She doesn't apologize; she doesn't explain; she socially manages the situation by making it worse in a different direction. The confidence with which she volunteers Fleabag's feelings is both rude to Fleabag and utterly oblivious. Perfect character moment. Earns 8+ for character integration and economy.

jokes.text: Fleabag (apparently high, looking at her feet): 'Oh, my God, look at my elegant feet.'
jokes.setup: They are high after the neighbour interaction
jokes.punchline: The stoned epiphany about one's own feet — the specific word 'elegant' applied to feet while high — a private comic observation shared with the audience
jokes.explanation: 'Elegant' is the perfect word choice — it's formal, slightly pretentious, and completely unexpected applied to feet in a stoned observation. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Fleabag sees someone and repeatedly shouts 'Hey. Hey! Hey. Hey. Hello! Hey. HEY!' escalating without response
jokes.setup: Fleabag spots someone she apparently recognizes and wants to get their attention
jokes.punchline: The escalation of 'Hey' through multiple intensities without apparent success — the comedy is the increasing desperation
jokes.explanation: The visual/audio comedy of escalating 'Hey' is inherently performative and loses without the delivery. Works as texture for the scene.

jokes.text: Man from the tube: 'Coming from you?' — he's telling Fleabag 'Easy, tiger' after she apparently almost ran into him, when HE is the one who got her coat caught outside the Tube
jokes.setup: A man tells Fleabag to slow down
jokes.punchline: His 'coming from you' implies she does this kind of thing (coat incidents etc.) regularly — and it turns out he's the man from the coat story she hasn't told yet
jokes.explanation: The pre-story of the coat incident is being set up. Functional but dependent on what follows.

jokes.text: Martin's relationship advice: 'Just fuck her, please, for the love of a good woman. Just wrap your willy up in a bow and just screw her, she's going insane.'
jokes.setup: Discussion about Claire's apparent frustration
jokes.punchline: 'Wrap your willy up in a bow' — Martin's solution to relationship problems, delivered as sober wisdom
jokes.explanation: 'Wrap your willy up in a bow' is a good absurdist image — it's very specific and visual. Martin dispensing sexual advice is funny because everything he does sexually is inappropriate, so his advice being 'just have more sex' delivered with the gravity of a relationship counsellor is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Date (Jake) explaining his lateness: 'I'd like to say I was trying to save a puppy, or something, but I just got my coat caught on someone else's coat outside the Tube. When we went to separate ourselves, it was fucking intense — I had to give him my coat.'
jokes.setup: Jake apologizes for being late with an explanation that starts modestly
jokes.punchline: The coat-entanglement was so 'intense' he had to surrender his coat to resolve it — a bizarre social transaction that reveals Jake's conflict-avoidance personality immediately
jokes.explanation: This joke is exceptional on multiple levels. First, the coat story is intrinsically absurd: coat-entanglement resolving via coat-surrendering is a specific, believable, and completely ridiculous social transaction. Second, the story instantly character-defines Jake as someone who gives away his coat to avoid conflict. Third, the reveal that he IS the man Fleabag nearly collided with (id 47) adds a retroactive layer. The choice of 'it was fucking intense' to describe coat separation is perfect incongruity. Earns 8+ for originality and character integration.

jokes.text: In a sex shop: Fleabag asks 'What are you craving?' and Fleabag says 'Oh, just a really, really cheap thrill.'
jokes.setup: Shop assistant presumably asks what she can help with
jokes.punchline: 'A really, really cheap thrill' — honest, self-aware, and slightly sad beneath the bravado
jokes.explanation: 'Really, really cheap' does the double work of comic self-deprecation and genuine revelation. The 'really, really' repetition is Fleabag emphasizing the low ambition of her desires.

jokes.text: In the sex shop: 'Hey, what are you craving?' / 'Oh, just a really, really cheap thrill.'
jokes.setup: Standard retail greeting in a sex shop
jokes.punchline: 'Just a really, really cheap thrill' — the shop assistant has presumably heard everything, and this is wearily honest
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the transparency — she's not even pretending it's for someone else, and the 'really, really' emphasis on 'cheap' is self-deprecating in the best way. Good Fleabag voice.

jokes.text: Fleabag clarifying the vibrator is 'for her sister. It's for my very sexually frustrated sister.'
jokes.setup: The shop assistant asks who the gift is for
jokes.punchline: Fleabag overshares the recipient's sexual frustration — using 'very sexually frustrated' as a product specification for her sister's gift
jokes.explanation: Callbacks on Claire's sexual frustration land well because the episode has established it carefully. 'Very sexually frustrated' as a product brief is funny and honest.

jokes.text: VIBRATOR BUZZES in the shop; the shop assistant says 'Oh. Oh, I think you just... do it at the bottom.' Then: 'It's all in the twist.'
jokes.setup: Someone (probably Jake) has accidentally activated a vibrator in the sex shop
jokes.punchline: The shop assistant's professional guidance — 'do it at the bottom... it's all in the twist' — delivered with complete expertise and calm while a vibrator buzzes unexpectedly
jokes.explanation: 'It's all in the twist' delivered with professional calm about a vibrator is a nice deadpan exchange. The physical comedy of accidentally activating shop merchandise is a reliable setup, elevated by the shopkeeper's serenity.

jokes.text: 'You should totally get one of those.' 'A vagina?' 'Yeah.' 'Oh, I've already got one.' 'Really? You have...No...You've got one?' 'I take it with me everywhere.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag recommends the vibrator to Jake
jokes.punchline: Jake mishears 'one of those [vibrators]' as 'a vagina' — or pretends to — leading to Fleabag's deadpan 'I take it with me everywhere' and the escalation to 'Where? Where's my vagina?'
jokes.explanation: The exchange is a beautifully structured piece of flirtation-as-wordplay. The misunderstanding (real or performed) allows Fleabag to play it completely straight: 'I take it with me everywhere' as a factual statement about anatomy. 'You don't have one on you now?' / 'Yep. Never going to get it.' creates charming sexual tension through absurdist logic. Earns 8+ for structure and the layered comic/flirtatious function it serves.

jokes.text: Shop assistant: 'OK, so this one's really great. It's half price because it's quite relentless. It's called the Burrower. Basically it doesn't stop until you've cum.'
jokes.setup: Shop assistant recommends a vibrator
jokes.punchline: 'It's half price because it's quite relentless' — the pricing logic suggests the relentlessness is a defect rather than a feature, and the clinical delivery of 'doesn't stop until you've cum' is perfect
jokes.explanation: Exceptional retail comedy. 'Half price because it's quite relentless' is the exact comic construction of a warranty disclosure — the relentlessness has reduced its retail value. The product name 'the Burrower' followed by 'doesn't stop until you've cum' delivered with shop-assistant matter-of-factness is outstanding. The comedy comes from the extreme product description in a customer-service register. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Jake in the car: 'Oh, my God, I love surprise parties. I love them, I love them, I love them.' Beat. 'Will your parents be there?' 'My dad might be...' 'Oh, intense.' 'Parents adore me.' 'I want you to be totally in love with me by the end of the night. OK?'
jokes.setup: Jake is enthusiastic about the party and tries to establish romantic intent
jokes.punchline: 'I want you to be totally in love with me by the end of the night. OK?' — stated as a reasonable logistical goal, with the 'OK?' seeking procedural confirmation
jokes.explanation: Jake's declaration of romantic intent framed as a project objective — with the 'OK?' seeking agreement — is a charming, funny, confident gambit. The escalation from 'I love surprise parties' to 'intense' to 'parents adore me' to 'totally in love with me' is well-structured.

jokes.text: At the party: Godmother says about a man: 'What a lovely husband you have.' Claire: 'Where is he then?' Martin is absent. The beat.
jokes.setup: Godmother praises Claire's husband at her own birthday party
jokes.punchline: Martin isn't there — the absence is the punchline, and Claire's 'Ah. Busy.' is a mortifying cover
jokes.explanation: 'Ah. Busy.' is a devastating British understatement — her husband isn't at her birthday party and she's covering with one syllable of dignity. Strong character and cringe beat.

jokes.text: Jake to a party guest: 'Thank you for asking, yeah, I'm essentially a documentary maker. Docs.' RAUCOUS LAUGH from the guest.
jokes.setup: Jake introduces himself professionally
jokes.punchline: The guest laughs uproariously at 'Docs' — implying Jake's self-description or manner is somehow genuinely funny in context, while Jake plays it completely straight
jokes.explanation: Jake's obliviousness to why he's being laughed at while delivering his professional credentials with sincerity is funny. The RAUCOUS LAUGH stage direction amplifies the absurdity.

jokes.text: Jake: 'I met her on a bus. So easy to pick up girls these days. I was like, "Hi", and she was like, "Take my number."' Another guest immediately leaves to try other wine.
jokes.setup: Jake charmingly describes meeting Fleabag
jokes.punchline: The guest's immediate exit — 'I'm just going to see if there are any other wines to try' — as an escape from Jake's company
jokes.explanation: Jake's oblivious enthusiasm clearing the room is a running gag — people quietly escaping his company. The wine excuse is charmingly transparent.

jokes.text: Two separate guests both pretend to be going for more wine to escape the date's conversation: 'I'm just going to see if there are any other wines to try.' / 'It's lovely, I'm just going to see if there are some others.'
jokes.setup: The date is being insufferable about his 'documentary making'
jokes.punchline: Two people use the identical wine excuse to escape, seconds apart — the repetition makes the second one funnier
jokes.explanation: The repetition structure is the joke. One person escaping via wine excuse is normal; the second person immediately following with the same excuse shows the universal rejection. Good structural comedy.

jokes.text: Party guest to Fleabag: 'Oh, I was so sorry to hear about Harry. Lovely Harry. Love Harry. Exciting news about his new job. Very exciting.' Fleabag: 'Yes, very exciting.' Guest: 'I was so pleased that you'd found someone else so fast.'
jokes.setup: A party guest ostensibly offers condolences about the breakup
jokes.punchline: The 'sympathy' is actually about Harry ('Love Harry', 'exciting news about his job') with Fleabag's recovery treated as suspicious speed — the guest prefers Harry
jokes.explanation: The social hypocrisy of the party-condolence is expertly observed — the guest's enthusiasm for Harry while nominally comforting Fleabag is a very specific kind of social cruelty. Fleabag's 'Yes, very exciting' is a perfect deadpan mask.

jokes.text: 'I just can't stop conjuring an image of you sitting around that cafe, just all alone, feeling so terribly lonely. Just can't stop picturing it.'
jokes.setup: The guest has been faux-sympathetic
jokes.punchline: She says 'just can't stop picturing it' twice — the image of Fleabag's loneliness is clearly giving her pleasure, not pain. The cruelty is in the repetition and the relish.
jokes.explanation: The repeated 'just can't stop picturing it' is beautifully written social cruelty — she's savouring the image. The combination of sympathy-language with obvious pleasure in Fleabag's suffering is precise and uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'I don't think you have to be alone to be lonely. Dad always taught me that.'
jokes.setup: The guest has been picturing Fleabag alone
jokes.punchline: Fleabag counters with a profundity attributed to Dad — the wisdom itself is genuine but the attribution to 'Dad' domesticates it comically, and it subtly devastates the guest
jokes.explanation: The line does double work: genuine emotional truth (Fleabag IS alone even with people around) and a social riposte that silences the guest. 'Dad always taught me that' gives it an oddly touching authority.

jokes.text: The Godmother mentions a missing artwork piece to Fleabag; Fleabag says 'That's awful, I'm so sorry.' Godmother: 'So sweet of you. Very sweet.' — with obvious sarcasm
jokes.setup: Godmother drops the information about the missing artwork
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's performed sympathy is met with Godmother's loaded 'So sweet of you. Very sweet' — implying Godmother knows or suspects Fleabag's involvement
jokes.explanation: The repeated 'sweet' signals the Godmother's knowledge or suspicion. The tension underneath the surface-level politeness is excellent cringe comedy — both women playing a social game with stakes beneath.

jokes.text: 'You are the most perfect-looking pair.' — said about Fleabag and the date, pointedly in front of the Godmother/party
jokes.setup: The date and Fleabag are at the party together
jokes.punchline: The compliment is double-edged — it reads as genuine but also puts the Godmother's nose slightly out of joint
jokes.explanation: Light moment, more social texture than a joke with real comedic punch.

jokes.text: GLASS SMASHES. Jake: 'Sorry, nothing happened. I was trying to be sexy.' Fleabag: 'It was, it was really sexy.' Jake: 'Was it?'
jokes.setup: Jake is apparently attempting a sexy gesture at the party
jokes.punchline: He breaks something in the attempt, apologizes, explains his intention, and is immediately, generously lied to — and then genuinely believes her
jokes.explanation: 'Sorry, nothing happened. I was trying to be sexy' is a perfect sequence of embarrassed damage control. His genuine 'Was it?' in response to her reassurance is endearing — he needs to believe it. The moment characterizes their fledgling connection well.

jokes.text: 'Claire, this is my friend...' 'Yes, yes, we've already met.' 'My wife.' — the man they've apparently been meeting is Martin's friend, who is also married to someone at the party
jokes.setup: Introduction of a man to Claire at the party
jokes.punchline: Claire has 'already met' him — implying some prior connection — followed by the reveal 'My wife' which lands as a small bomb
jokes.explanation: 'My wife' as a two-word scene-ender lands with good economy. The implication is there's more history here than we know. Rewatch bonus for the implied backstory it hints at.

jokes.text: Martin reveals his present: a sculpture/statue of Claire's body. 'It is a shrine to your body... because I love your body.' The guests' reactions of 'This is really quite something' and the attempt to hide it.
jokes.setup: Martin presents Claire's birthday present
jokes.punchline: A body shrine — a statue of his wife's body — presented publicly at a business birthday party. The escalating social horror as guests react and someone moves to put it 'somewhere safe'
jokes.explanation: The body shrine at a business party is an extraordinary cringe comedy set-piece. Martin's earnest 'I love your body' delivered with sincerity at a professional event, the guests' polite horror ('probably a bit inappropriate for your guests to see your body'), the practical solution of putting it 'somewhere safe' — all layers work. Earns 8+ for originality of the object itself and the sustained escalating discomfort it creates.

jokes.text: Fleabag to the Godmother: 'Smooth. I told you I'd find you a buyer.'
jokes.setup: The body statue has just been publicly displayed and caused embarrassment
jokes.punchline: Fleabag congratulates herself on finding a buyer for an artwork using a birthday party as the sales vehicle — revealing she sold the statue knowing it would be given as this gift
jokes.explanation: The reveal that the 'missing artwork' the Godmother mentioned was sold by Fleabag to Martin as Claire's birthday present is a satisfying twist. The Godmother's 'missing piece' and Fleabag's guilty 'That's awful, I'm so sorry' at 17:34 are now retroactively much funnier. Earns 8 for the payoff structure and character integration — Fleabag's chaos has a specific shape.

jokes.text: Godmother: 'You'd fuck anything, wouldn't you?'
jokes.setup: The Godmother is making an observation about Fleabag's dates/choices
jokes.punchline: The bluntness of 'you'd fuck anything' from a woman who is ostensibly a family figure — delivered apparently without malice or context
jokes.explanation: The Godmother's cheerful lewdness toward Fleabag is characteristic of their dynamic. The frank observation delivered without social filter is funny for its bluntness.

jokes.text: Martin approaches Fleabag: 'How much do you want for it?' 'Finger up the ass, nipple tickle...?'
jokes.setup: Martin is apparently negotiating something with Fleabag
jokes.punchline: Martin proposes sexual acts as currency — the 'finger up the ass, nipple tickle' as an itemized payment offer is horrifically specific and characteristic
jokes.explanation: Martin offering sexual acts as currency continues his established pattern of treating sex as a casual transactional good. The specificity of 'finger up the ass, nipple tickle' as a priced menu is his most grotesque iteration yet. Escalation from the earlier bathroom offer.

jokes.text: Fleabag to Martin: 'She's going to leave you one day. You looking forward to that?'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been subjected to Martin's advances all episode
jokes.punchline: The retaliatory prediction of Claire leaving him — delivered as a quiet bomb rather than a comic punchline — dark comedy in the mode of revenge
jokes.explanation: The 'You looking forward to that?' is the comedic fuse — turning a devastating prediction into a genuine question. It's funny because it's true and delivered with complete calm. Fleabag as someone who uses honesty as a weapon.

jokes.text: A man approaches Fleabag: 'I think you took my coat.' She resists. He insists. It's the Tube man from earlier.
jokes.setup: Jake mentioned he gave his coat away to a man outside the Tube; the earlier 'Easy, tiger' man appeared briefly
jokes.punchline: The coat story resolves: the coat was passed to Fleabag somehow, completing a coat-chain that is absurdly specific
jokes.explanation: The coat-chain callback — Jake gave his coat to the Tube man, who is now at the party retrieving it from Fleabag — is a neatly constructed running gag that pays off with physical evidence. Rewatch bonus for tracing the chain.

jokes.text: Fleabag gives Claire the Burrower: 'It's called a Burrower — it basically won't stop until you cum.' Claire: 'Sounds horrendous. Thank you.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag gifts Claire the vibrator from the sex shop
jokes.punchline: 'Sounds horrendous. Thank you.' — Claire's response is perfectly in character: the revulsion and the gratitude delivered in the same breath, both completely sincere
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. 'Sounds horrendous. Thank you.' is a near-perfect line. It is entirely, exclusively Claire: her disgust at the explicit product description, and her genuine gratitude that someone understands what she needs — both real and simultaneous. The economy is exceptional (four words), the character integration is perfect (only Claire says this), and it pays off both the sex shop scene and the entire episode's thesis about Claire's repressed desire. Approaches the Snip Snap anchor in character specificity and emotional economy.

jokes.text: Claire's birthday business verdict: 'Huge. You know, I don't want to jinx anything, but huge. Could be life-changing.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks if the birthday-business was good
jokes.punchline: Claire's unironic 'life-changing' assessment of a business outcome at her own birthday party — the business meeting framing of her birthday has paid off for her
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the episode's opening characterisation of Claire's birthday. The irony that she was right — it WAS a business meeting — and it worked — is a satisfying structural close on that thread.

jokes.text: Claire asks 'Are you OK?' Fleabag: 'Yeah.' Claire: 'Tell the truth.'
jokes.setup: Party is ending; surface-level exchange of pleasantries
jokes.punchline: Claire's 'Tell the truth' — two words that puncture Fleabag's habitual deflection, showing Claire knows exactly what she's doing
jokes.explanation: This is a small but exceptional moment. 'Tell the truth' cuts through Fleabag's entire performative apparatus in two words. The comedy is in the precision — Claire sees through her completely — and it simultaneously deepens the dramatic stakes. The joke is that Fleabag, who breaks the fourth wall to tell us the truth, cannot tell the truth to her sister. Earns 8+ for economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Post-sex: Jake says 'Surprisingly bony.' Fleabag: 'I'm nearly finished. I'm nearly finished.' Jake: 'It's like having sex with a protractor.'
jokes.setup: Post-coital exchange; Jake makes a physical observation about Fleabag
jokes.punchline: 'It's like having sex with a protractor' — the specificity of 'protractor' over any other sharp geometric instrument is the joke; a protractor is a comedy word and a very specific complaint
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. 'Protractor' is a perfect comedy word — specific, unexpectedly educational, and slightly absurd applied to a human body. The setup of Fleabag saying 'I'm nearly finished' mid-sex and being described as bony adds layers. 'Having sex with a protractor' is highly quotable and inventive. Earns 8+ for originality and economy.

jokes.text: Post-sex: both keep saying 'That was amazing. Yeah. That was amazing. Yeah.' escalating in decreasing conviction, repeated four times across 10 seconds.
jokes.setup: Sex has concluded; standard post-coital reassurance exchange begins
jokes.punchline: The mechanical repetition of 'that was amazing / yeah' becomes increasingly hollow — the protocol is running but the meaning has drained from it
jokes.explanation: The repetition is the joke — the more they say it, the less it means. A nice structural comedy beat about the performance of post-sex satisfaction.

jokes.text: Jake: 'For fuck's sake.' Fleabag: 'What?' Jake: 'OK, you don't go through life with teeth like these and not know when someone's pretending.'
jokes.setup: After the 'that was amazing' exchange has run its course
jokes.punchline: 'You don't go through life with teeth like these' — Jake uses his own cosmetic vulnerability (his teeth) as the credential for detecting performed satisfaction — an absurd but oddly logical claim
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best line and one of the best individual lines in the series. 'You don't go through life with teeth like these and not know when someone's pretending' is extraordinary for multiple reasons: (1) It's entirely character-specific — Jake's self-awareness about his unconventional teeth is both self-deprecating and the basis of his emotional intelligence; (2) The logic is airtight yet absurd: having bad teeth makes you an expert in detecting performed reactions; (3) It subverts the 'amazing' sequence by revealing he already knew; (4) It's immediately quotable and applicable far beyond the scene; (5) It does simultaneous emotional/comedic work — he's hurt but delivering the hurt with wit. This is comparable to the Snip Snap anchor in character specificity and structural precision, and surpasses it in linguistic originality.

jokes.text: Jake: 'What the fuck is that?! I'll kick it.' Fleabag: 'No, no.' Jake: 'I'll kick it.'
jokes.setup: Something moves in the room — later revealed to be the guinea pig
jokes.punchline: Jake's immediate threat to kick whatever it is — no investigation, pure threat — is a funny instinctive response
jokes.explanation: 'I'll kick it' twice is funny for its confidence and immediacy. Jake's anti-rodent instinct is a light character beat.

jokes.text: GUINEA PIG SQUEAKS — final sound of the episode, after the 'that is a rat' exchange
jokes.setup: Jake has firmly categorized the guinea pig as a rat
jokes.punchline: The guinea pig squeaks — either an objection or just being a guinea pig — as the final beat of the episode, a tiny animal having the last word
jokes.explanation: The guinea pig squeak as episode-ender is a perfect piece of comic economy. After all the human complexity, grief, bad sex, and social disaster, the final word belongs to the depressed guinea pig who 'has death in its eyes.' This closes the episode's running thread with elegant simplicity and just enough melancholy to register as tonally perfect for Fleabag.

tmdb_overview: Fleabag helps her inappropriate brother-in-law buy a present for Claire, who is organising her own surprise birthday party. Meanwhile, on the hunt for a date, Fleabag reconnects with a toothy friend.
